summary: Fowler states his First Law of Distributed Object Design with characteristic brevity: don't distribute your objects. This pithy rule, drawn from his Patterns of Enterprise Application Architecture book, warns against the common architectural mistake of splitting objects across network boundaries unnecessarily. The entire post is essentially a memorable one-liner meant to caution architects against over-engineering distributed systems.
key_points: Distribution of objects across network boundaries should be avoided when possible
This principle is foundational enough to be called a 'First Law'
The full reasoning is elaborated in Patterns of Enterprise Application Architecture
money_quotes: My First Law of Distributed Object Design: Don't distribute your objects.
key_insight: The complexity and performance costs of distributing objects across network boundaries usually outweigh the benefits, so keep objects local unless you have compelling reasons to distribute them.
